skill.name: dbos-typescript
skill.description: DBOS TypeScript SDK for building reliable, fault-tolerant applications with durable workflows. Use this skill when writing TypeScript code with DBOS, creating workflows and steps, using queues, usi...
skill.summary: Build reliable, fault-tolerant TypeScript applications with DBOS durable workflows
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: This is a legitimate documentation skill for DBOS TypeScript SDK. Static findings are false positives triggered by markdown syntax (code blocks using backticks), standard configuration patterns (environment variables), and documentation keywords. No actual security vulnerabilities present.

medium_findings.title: False Positive: External Commands Detection
medium_findings.description: Scanner detected 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' in 350 locations. These are markdown code blocks using backticks (```), not actual shell commands. This is a false positive pattern matching issue.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Code fences in markdown (```typescript) are incorrectly flagged as shell backticks. No actual shell execution in this documentation skill.

low_findings.title: Environment Variable Access (Legitimate)
low_findings.description: DBOS applications use environment variables for configuration (e.g., DBOS_SYSTEM_DATABASE_URL). This is standard configuration practice, not a security issue.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Standard environment variable configuration for database connection strings is legitimate and recommended practice.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs (Documentation)
low_findings.description: Documentation URLs (https://docs.dbos.dev/) are hardcoded. These are legitimate reference links, not security issues.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Documentation links are appropriate and do not pose security risks.

content.value_statement: DBOS TypeScript SDK enables developers to build reliable applications with durable workflows that automatically recover from failures. The skill provides best practices for workflows, steps, queues, and communication patterns.
actual_capabilities: Guide DBOS workflow and step creation with proper patterns
Configure DBOS applications with lifecycle management
Implement queue-based concurrency with rate limiting
Set up workflow communication via events, messages, and streams
Apply idempotency and determinism patterns for reliable recovery
Test DBOS applications with proper mocking strategies
limitations: Does not include actual DBOS SDK runtime code
Does not provide database connection management
Does not cover deployment or infrastructure setup
Focuses on patterns rather than complete application examples

use_cases.title: Build resilient data processing pipelines
use_cases.description: Create workflows that reliably process data with automatic retry and recovery from failures

use_cases.title: Implement reliable microservice communication
use_cases.description: Use DBOS events and messages for decoupled, fault-tolerant inter-service communication

use_cases.title: Add durability to existing Node.js applications
use_cases.description: Enhance TypeScript applications with durable workflow execution that survives crashes and restarts

prompt_templates.title: Create a basic DBOS workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a DBOS workflow that fetches data from an API and processes it. Show how to use DBOS.runStep for the API call and register the workflow properly.

prompt_templates.title: Implement workflow with retry logic
prompt_templates.prompt: Show how to configure step retries with exponential backoff for a DBOS workflow that calls an unreliable external service.

prompt_templates.title: Set up workflow queues
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a DBOS queue with concurrency limits and rate limiting for processing items. Show how to enqueue workflows and configure worker behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Implement workflow communication
prompt_templates.prompt: Demonstrate how to use DBOS events for inter-workflow communication. Show how one workflow can emit events and another can listen for them.

output_examples.input: How do I create a DBOS workflow that calls an API?
output: Use DBOS.runStep to wrap the API call, then register the workflow with DBOS.registerWorkflow. Steps are checkpointed for reliable recovery.

output_examples.input: What's the difference between a workflow and a step?
output: Workflows must be deterministic and handle orchestration. Steps perform non-deterministic operations like API calls, file I/O, and database queries.

best_practices: Keep workflows deterministic - move all non-deterministic operations to steps
Always use DBOS.runStep for any operation that accesses external services or databases
Configure appropriate retry policies for steps that may fail transiently
anti_patterns: Calling workflows from within steps - this breaks the execution model
Using Math.random() or Date.now() directly in workflows - they cause non-determinism
Modifying global variables from workflows - this breaks replay capability

faq.question: What is DBOS?
faq.answer: DBOS (Database-Oriented Operating System) is a framework that provides durable workflow execution for TypeScript applications, ensuring reliability and automatic recovery from failures.

faq.question: How does DBOS ensure reliability?
faq.answer: DBOS checkpointing records workflow state, allowing automatic replay and recovery after crashes or restarts without data loss.

faq.question: When should I use a step vs a workflow?
faq.answer: Use workflows for orchestration and decision-making. Use steps for operations that are non-deterministic, like API calls, file operations, or database queries.

faq.question: Can I call external APIs from workflows?
faq.answer: No, external API calls must be wrapped in DBOS.runStep. Direct API calls in workflows break determinism and recovery.

faq.question: How do I handle failures in DBOS?
faq.answer: Configure retry policies on steps using the retries option in runStep. Workflows automatically retry from the last checkpoint on failure.

faq.question: Does DBOS work with any database?
faq.answer: DBOS supports PostgreSQL, MySQL, and SQLite databases through its transaction system.
